summary: Demonstrated how automated W2S researchers exhibit optimism bias when repeatedly querying held-out validation sets. Quantified 2.0pp bias after 750 submissions and proposed concrete protocols (submission budgets, holdout rotation) to bound the leakage.
claims: Repeated submission to held-out validation inflates apparent W2S progress by 2.0 percentage points over 750 evaluations
Optimism bias emerges sharply around 50 submissions, with bias ratio reaching 300%
Bias causes apparent improvement (+1.2%) while actual test performance degrades (-0.8%)
Submission budget limits of 50 evaluations per held-out set can bound optimism bias
Holdout rotation across 3 fresh splits reduces prolonged exposure to any single validation set

evidence.summary: Main experiment showing 2.0pp optimism bias over 50 iterations with 15 methods each
Submission budget analysis showing bias threshold at 50 submissions

limitations: Synthetic task may not reflect real W2S generalization patterns
Simple method variants may not capture complex W2S approach diversity
Fixed data splits rather than dynamic splitting protocols
Gaussian noise model may not match real method uncertainty
Single accuracy metric rather than multi-objective optimization
next: Validate on real W2S benchmarks with larger language models
Test bias patterns across diverse W2S methods beyond simple hyperparameter variants
Develop adaptive submission budgets based on real-time bias estimation
Implement meta-learning for automated researchers to detect their own overfitting
